lessons.title: Question tags â€” â€¦isn't it? â€¦aren't you?
lessons.description: Learn to use question tags to check information, invite agreement, and sound natural in conversation.

content.title: What are question tags?
content.body:
A QUESTION TAG is a short question added to the end of a statement. We use them to:
â€¢ Check information we think is true
â€¢ Invite the other person to agree
â€¢ Start or keep a conversation going

The rule:
POSITIVE statement â†’ NEGATIVE tag
'It's a beautiful day, isn't it?'

NEGATIVE statement â†’ POSITIVE tag
'You're not from here, are you?'

More examples:
â€¢ 'She works in IT, doesn't she?'
â€¢ 'They didn't come, did they?'
â€¢ 'You've been to Goa, haven't you?'
â€¢ 'He's very talented, isn't he?'
content.grammar_note:
Tag = auxiliary verb + pronoun
Positive â†’ negative tag: is â†’ isn't, do â†’ don't, have â†’ haven't
Negative â†’ positive tag: isn't â†’ is, don't â†’ do

content.title: Question tags â€” tense matching
content.body:
The tag verb must MATCH the main verb tense:

Present simple:
â€¢ 'She works here, doesn't she?'
â€¢ 'They live in Mumbai, don't they?'

Present continuous:
â€¢ 'You're studying for the exam, aren't you?'

Past simple:
â€¢ 'He called you, didn't he?'
â€¢ 'They didn't come, did they?'

Present perfect:
â€¢ 'You've met her before, haven't you?'

Modal verbs:
â€¢ 'She can swim, can't she?'
â€¢ 'You'll be there, won't you?'
â€¢ 'They should know, shouldn't they?'

Special case â€” 'I am':
â€¢ 'I'm next, aren't I?' (NOT 'amn't I')
content.grammar_note:
Match the tense of the main clause
Use the SAME auxiliary in the tag
I am â†’ aren't I (special case)

content.title: Vocabulary: Conversation starters

vocabulary.meaning: to check that something is correct
vocabulary.example: You're Priya's friend, aren't you?

vocabulary.meaning: to ask someone to say they agree
vocabulary.example: Great weather today, isn't it?

vocabulary.meaning: to make sure something is true
vocabulary.example: The train leaves at 6, doesn't it?

vocabulary.meaning: a short question added to a statement
vocabulary.example: 'It's lovely here, isn't it?' â€” 'isn't it' is the tag.

vocabulary.meaning: the rise and fall of your voice
vocabulary.example: Rising intonation = genuine question. Falling = seeking agreement.

vocabulary.meaning: asking because you truly don't know
vocabulary.example: You haven't met him, have you? (I'm not sure)

sentences: It's a beautiful evening, isn't it?
You work at TCS, don't you?
She hasn't called back yet, has she?
They were at the concert, weren't they?
You can drive, can't you?

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 1: Friends catching up at a cafÃ© â€” Chennai
content.setting: Priya and Kavya are old college friends who haven't met in a year. They meet at a cafÃ© in Chennai. This dialogue is full of question tags used naturally in conversation.
content.learning_note: Listen for how question tags are used to CHECK information, INVITE AGREEMENT, and KEEP THE CONVERSATION GOING. You will practise this dialogue again in Lesson 13.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Kavya! Oh my goodness â€” it's been so long, hasn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I know! Almost a year, isn't it? You look wonderful, by the way.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Thank you! You haven't changed at all, have you? Still the same Kavya!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! Well, a lot has changed actually. You know I moved to Bangalore, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes! You're working at that tech company, aren't you? TechNova or something?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: TechBridge â€” close enough! It's going really well. It's a bit hectic though, isn't it always?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Ha, yes. You were always a hard worker, weren't you? Even in college.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: You weren't so bad yourself! You're still teaching, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes, still at the same school. Different class this year though. You haven't tried this cafÃ© before, have you?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: No â€” first time! The filter coffee here is amazing, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: The best in Adyar, I think. Your family is still in Coimbatore, aren't they?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, my parents are. But my sister got married and moved to Dubai â€” you remember her, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course! She was in the year below us, wasn't she? How lovely! We should catch up properly â€” lunch next time, shouldn't we?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Absolutely. It's long overdue, isn't it? I'll be back in Chennai next month â€” we'll plan something then!

content.repeat_speaking_note: In Lesson 13, you will: (1) listen and repeat this dialogue, (2) answer comprehension questions, (3) identify all question tags used, and (4) role-play it with a partner.

Where does Kavya work?

content.instruction: Find and correct the question tag mistake.

sentences.wrong: She works in the hospital, isn't she?
sentences.correct: She works in the hospital, doesn't she?
sentences.explanation: Simple present 'works' â†’ tag uses DO/DOES: 'doesn't she'. 'Isn't' is for the verb TO BE.

sentences.wrong: You've been to Mysore, have you?
sentences.correct: You've been to Mysore, haven't you?
sentences.explanation: Positive statement (have been) â†’ NEGATIVE tag (haven't you).

sentences.wrong: They didn't call, didn't they?
sentences.correct: They didn't call, did they?
sentences.explanation: Negative statement (didn't) â†’ POSITIVE tag (did they).

content.scenario: You meet someone at a friend's wedding. You think you've seen them at the gym but aren't 100% sure. What do you say to start a conversation?
options: Do you go to the gym?
You go to the gym, don't you? I think I've seen you there.
I saw you at the gym, didn't I not?
content.correct: You go to the gym, don't you? I think I've seen you there.
content.explanation: A question tag is perfect here â€” you're not 100% sure, so you're checking while starting a conversation naturally.

content.instruction: Use question tags to have a short conversation. Imagine you've just met someone at a friend's gathering. Use question tags to check things you think are true about them.
content.prompt: Create 6 sentences using question tags in a natural conversation starter. Use different tenses.
content.example_response: Nice to meet you! You're Meera's colleague, aren't you? I thought I recognised you from her photos. You work in the design team, don't you? I remember her mentioning someone from Hyderabad. You're originally from Hyderabad, aren't you? It must be a big change coming to Chennai â€” the weather's different, isn't it? Much more humid! You've been here a few months, haven't you? You'll get used to the summers eventually â€” everyone does, don't they? Ha! Anyway, it's lovely to meet you â€” Meera's mentioned you a lot, hasn't she? Only good things, I promise!

content.instruction: Read the Scenario 1 dialogue again and answer these questions in your own words.
content.prompt:
Answer these questions based on the CafÃ© Scenario dialogue:
1. Why are Priya and Kavya meeting?
2. What has changed in Kavya's life since they last met?
3. What question tags do you notice in the conversation? Give 3 examples.
4. How do they plan to meet next time?
content.example_response: Priya and Kavya are meeting because they haven't seen each other for almost a year â€” they're old college friends who have stayed in touch but haven't had a chance to sit down and catch up properly. A lot has changed in Kavya's life since they last met. She has moved to Bangalore and started working at a company called TechBridge. Her sister also got married and moved to Dubai. As for question tags â€” there are many! For example: 'it's been so long, hasn't it?' â€” checking if they agree it's been a long time. 'You're working at TechNova, aren't you?' â€” Priya is checking what she thinks she knows but isn't sure about. 'You haven't tried this cafÃ© before, have you?' â€” a genuine check because Kavya just arrived. They plan to meet for lunch next time Kavya is in Chennai, which will be next month.

content.title: Lesson 1 complete â€” Question tags
key_points: Positive statement â†’ NEGATIVE tag
Negative statement â†’ POSITIVE tag
Tag = auxiliary + pronoun â€” must match the main verb tense
I am â†’ aren't I (special case)
Rising intonation = genuine question; Falling intonation = seeking agreement
Use tags to check info, invite agreement, and keep conversation going
content.next_lesson: So do I / Neither do I â€” agreeing and disagreeing

lessons.title: So do I / Neither do I â€” agreeing in conversation
lessons.description: Use 'so' and 'neither/nor' to agree with positive and negative statements naturally in conversation.

content.title: Agreeing with positive statements â€” So + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something POSITIVE and you agree â€” you feel the same:

Use: SO + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
â€¢ 'I love filter coffee.' â†’ 'So do I!'
â€¢ 'I'm really tired today.' â†’ 'So am I!'
â€¢ 'I was at the match last night.' â†’ 'So was I!'
â€¢ 'I've been to Goa.' â†’ 'So have I!'
â€¢ 'I'll be at the meeting.' â†’ 'So will I!'
â€¢ 'I can speak Telugu.' â†’ 'So can I!'

The auxiliary verb MATCHES the original statement.
content.grammar_note:
SO + auxiliary + I/we/she/he/they
Match the tense and modal: loveâ†’do, amâ†’am, wasâ†’was, canâ†’can

content.title: Agreeing with negative statements â€” Neither/Nor + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something NEGATIVE and you feel the same:

Use: NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
â€¢ 'I don't like spicy food.' â†’ 'Neither do I.' / 'Nor do I.'
â€¢ 'I'm not ready yet.' â†’ 'Neither am I.'
â€¢ 'I haven't been to the North-East.' â†’ 'Neither have I.'
â€¢ 'I can't swim.' â†’ 'Neither can I.'
â€¢ 'I won't be there.' â†’ 'Neither will I.'

NEITHER and NOR mean the same here â€” both are correct.
Alternative: 'Me neither.' (informal, very common in speech)
content.grammar_note:
NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary + I/she/he/they
'Me neither' = informal alternative
Word order: Neither do I (NOT Neither I do)

content.title: Disagreeing â€” expressing a different view
content.body:
When you DON'T agree (you feel differently):

Use: Actually, I do. / Actually, I don't.

Examples:
â€¢ 'I love cricket.' â†’ 'Really? I don't actually. I prefer football.'
â€¢ 'I don't like the heat.' â†’ 'Oh, I do! I love hot weather.'
â€¢ 'I'm not worried about the exam.' â†’ 'Oh, I am! I'm really nervous.'

Soften disagreement with:
â€¢ 'Really? I actually...' 
â€¢ 'That's interesting â€” I feel quite differently.'
â€¢ 'Oh, I'm the opposite â€” I...'

Never just say: 'No, wrong!' â€” always soften it.
content.grammar_note:
Disagreeing: 'Oh, I do/don't actually'
'Really? I'm the opposite â€” I...'
Always sound warm, not confrontational

sentences: I really enjoy long train journeys. â€” So do I! There's something relaxing about them.
I'm a bit nervous about the presentation. â€” So am I. Let's practise together.
I haven't tried Chettinad food yet. â€” Neither have I â€” we should go!
I can't stand traffic in the evening. â€” Neither can I. It's exhausting.
I love early mornings. â€” Really? I don't actually. I'm a night person!

content.question: Priya says: 'I'm really stressed about the deadline.' You feel the same. What do you say?
content.correct: So am I.
content.explanation: Positive statement with 'am' â†’ So + AM + I. 'So am I' = I am also stressed.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I don't drink coffee at all.' You also don't drink coffee. What do you say?
content.correct: Neither do I.
content.explanation: Negative statement (don't) â†’ NEITHER + DO + I. Word order: Neither + aux + subject.

content.question: Kavya says: 'I've never been to a cricket match.' Ravi has been to cricket matches. What does he say?
options: Really? I have! It's amazing â€” you must go.
content.correct: Really? I have! It's amazing â€” you must go.
content.explanation: Ravi DISAGREES â€” he HAS been. So he says 'Really? I have!' not 'So/Neither have I'.

content.question: Meera says: 'I can't cook at all!' Sonal also can't cook. What does Sonal say?
options: Neither can I â€” I survive on Swiggy!
content.correct: Neither can I â€” I survive on Swiggy!
content.explanation: Negative (can't) â†’ NEITHER + CAN + I. The added joke makes it sound natural!

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 2: Office colleagues agreeing and disagreeing â€” Bangalore
content.setting: Three colleagues â€” Ravi, Sonal, and Arjun â€” are having chai in the break room and chatting about work and life. This dialogue features So/Neither structures and natural agreement/disagreement.
content.learning_note: Notice how So do I / Neither do I / Me neither / Oh, I do! create natural flow. This dialogue is revisited in Lesson 13 for speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I've been really struggling with the new project management software. It's so complicated!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So have I! Nobody explained it properly, did they?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I actually find it quite intuitive. Maybe I can walk you both through it?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: That would be great. I also haven't taken my annual leave yet this year.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither have I! I keep saying I'll plan a trip and then something comes up.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Me neither, honestly. I haven't even looked at my leave balance in months.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I really need a holiday. I'm completely burnt out.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So am I. The last two months have been non-stop.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I don't feel too bad actually â€” I've been sleeping much better since I stopped working late.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh, I can't do that. I'm a night owl â€” I do my best work after 10 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: So do I! I can't concentrate during the day with all the noise.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I'm the opposite. I can't work at night at all. But we all get results in the end, don't we?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: True! By the way â€” do you both like the new canteen menu?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I love it! So much better than before.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: So do I â€” but I wish they had more vegetarian options.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither do I mind vegetarian â€” I've been trying to eat less meat recently anyway!

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.wrong: 'I love biryani.' â†’ 'So I do!'
sentences.correct: 'I love biryani.' â†’ 'So do I!'
sentences.explanation: Word order: SO + auxiliary + subject. 'So do I' â€” not 'So I do'.

sentences.wrong: 'I haven't seen that film.' â†’ 'Neither I have.'
sentences.correct: 'I haven't seen that film.' â†’ 'Neither have I.'
sentences.explanation: Word order: NEITHER + auxiliary + subject. 'Neither have I' â€” not 'Neither I have'.

sentences.wrong: 'I'm not worried.' â†’ 'Neither am not I.'
sentences.correct: 'I'm not worried.' â†’ 'Neither am I.'
sentences.explanation: NEITHER already contains the negative meaning â€” don't add 'not' again.

content.scenario: Your friend says: 'I've never tried sushi.' You also haven't tried sushi. What's the most natural response?
options: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.correct: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.explanation: NEITHER HAVE I is the correct structure. Adding a follow-up suggestion makes it feel like natural conversation.

content.instruction: Play the agreement game â€” respond to each statement below using So/Neither + the correct auxiliary. Then add a natural follow-up comment.
content.prompt:
Respond to these 5 statements with So/Neither + auxiliary. Then add one extra sentence each time:
1. 'I love street food.'
2. 'I'm not a fan of Mondays.'
3. 'I've been learning a new skill recently.'
4. 'I can't imagine living without my phone.'
5. 'I didn't sleep well last night.'
content.example_response: Let me respond to all five! First: 'I love street food' â€” So do I! There's a great pani puri stall near my office that I visit almost every evening. Second: 'I'm not a fan of Mondays' â€” Neither am I! Mondays always feel like such a slow start. I need at least two coffees to get going. Third: 'I've been learning a new skill recently' â€” So have I! I've been taking online classes for data visualisation â€” it's challenging but really interesting. Fourth: 'I can't imagine living without my phone' â€” Neither can I honestly. Everything is on there â€” work, family, navigation, entertainment. Fifth: 'I didn't sleep well last night' â€” Neither did I! I was lying awake thinking about the presentation today. Glad it went well in the end!

content.instruction: Read the Scenario 2 dialogue and answer these questions in your own words.
content.prompt:
Answer based on the office break room dialogue:
1. What do Ravi and Sonal have in common regarding software?
2. Why haven't any of them taken their annual leave?
3. What are Ravi and Sonal's working style â€” and how is Arjun's different?
4. What opinion do the three colleagues share about the new canteen?
content.example_response: In the office dialogue, Ravi and Sonal have something in common â€” they're both struggling with the new project management software. Neither of them found it easy to learn because it wasn't properly explained to them. Arjun, interestingly, is the exception â€” he finds it quite intuitive and even offers to help them. As for annual leave, none of them have taken it yet this year. They all keep planning to go on holiday but something always comes up and they end up postponing it. About working style â€” Ravi and Sonal are both night owls. They do their best work late at night and find it hard to concentrate during the day with office noise. Arjun is the opposite â€” he can't work at night at all and goes to sleep early. Regarding the canteen, all three seem to like the new menu. Ravi loves it and thinks it's much better than before. Arjun agrees but wishes there were more vegetarian options.

content.title: Lesson 2 complete â€” So do I / Neither do I
key_points: Agree with POSITIVE: SO + auxiliary + I (So do I / So am I / So have I)
Agree with NEGATIVE: NEITHER + auxiliary + I (Neither do I / Neither am I)
Word order: SO/NEITHER + auxiliary + subject (NOT So I do)
Informal: 'Me too' (positive) / 'Me neither' (negative)
Disagree politely: 'Really? I do / I don't actually...'
The auxiliary must MATCH the tense of the original statement
content.next_lesson: Indirect questions â€” Could you tell me whereâ€¦?

lessons.title: Indirect questions â€” Could you tell me whereâ€¦?
lessons.description: Ask questions more politely and formally using indirect question structures.

content.title: What is an indirect question?
content.body:
Direct questions are short and clear. Indirect questions are more POLITE â€” we use them with strangers, in formal situations, or when we want to sound less abrupt.

DIRECT â†’ INDIRECT:
â€¢ 'Where is the station?' â†’ 'Could you tell me where the station is?'
â€¢ 'What time does it close?' â†’ 'Do you know what time it closes?'
â€¢ 'Is there a pharmacy nearby?' â†’ 'Could you tell me if there's a pharmacy nearby?'
â€¢ 'When does the bus leave?' â†’ 'Do you have any idea when the bus leaves?'

Key change: The INDIRECT question uses STATEMENT word order â€” NOT question word order.
Direct: 'Where IS the station?' (inverted)
Indirect: '...where the station IS.' (normal)
content.grammar_note:
Indirect questions = statement word order inside
NO inversion after the indirect opener
NO auxiliary DO/DID in indirect wh- questions

content.title: Indirect question openers + if/whether
content.body:
Common indirect question OPENERS:
â€¢ Could you tell meâ€¦?
â€¢ Do you knowâ€¦?
â€¢ Do you have any ideaâ€¦?
â€¢ Would you mind telling meâ€¦?
â€¢ I was wondering ifâ€¦
â€¢ Can you tell meâ€¦?

For YES/NO questions â€” use IF or WHETHER:
Direct: 'Is the meeting on Friday?'
Indirect: 'Could you tell me IF/WHETHER the meeting is on Friday?'

Direct: 'Does she speak English?'
Indirect: 'Do you know IF she speaks English?'

WH- questions â€” use the same wh- word:
Direct: 'Where is the manager?'
Indirect: 'Could you tell me WHERE the manager is?'
content.grammar_note:
YES/NO â†’ indirect opener + IF/WHETHER
WH- â†’ indirect opener + wh- word
Statement word order always follows the opener

content.title: Vocabulary: Polite requests and service language

vocabulary.meaning: to ask for information formally
vocabulary.example: I'm calling to enquire about the job vacancy.

vocabulary.meaning: to pause before asking
vocabulary.example: Please don't hesitate to ask if you need help.

vocabulary.meaning: to make something clearer
vocabulary.example: Could you clarify what the process involves?

vocabulary.meaning: help
vocabulary.example: I need some assistance with this form.

vocabulary.meaning: straightforward, without being polite/indirect
vocabulary.example: The direct question was too blunt for the situation.

vocabulary.meaning: showing respect and good manners
vocabulary.example: An indirect question sounds more polite.

sentences: Could you tell me where the nearest ATM is?
Do you know if the office is open on Sundays?
I was wondering whether you could help me with this form.
Would you mind telling me what time the last train leaves?
Do you have any idea how long the wait will be?

content.question: Convert to indirect: 'Where is the post office?' Choose the correct version.
options: Could you tell me where is the post office?
Could you tell me where the post office is?
Could you tell me where does the post office is?
Do you know where is post office?
content.correct: Could you tell me where the post office is?

content.title: Indirect questions in a professional setting â€” hospital reception, Hyderabad
content.setting: Kavya is visiting a hospital for the first time to take her elderly neighbour. She uses indirect questions throughout to be polite.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Excuse me â€” could you tell me where the general outpatient department is?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Of course! It's on the second floor â€” take the lift on your left.

content.instruction: You are a tourist in a new city in India. You need to find out 5 things. Ask each question as an INDIRECT question to a local person.
content.prompt: Ask 5 indirect questions about: (1) the nearest metro station, (2) whether the museum is open today, (3) how long it takes to reach the beach, (4) where you can find good local food, (5) whether taxis are available nearby.
content.example_response: Excuse me â€” I hope you don't mind me asking! First â€” could you tell me where the nearest metro station is? I've been walking for a while and I can't seem to find it. Second â€” do you know if the city museum is open today? I read it might be closed on Mondays. Third â€” I was wondering how long it takes to reach the beach from here on foot or by auto. Fourth â€” could you tell me where I could find some good local food? I want to try authentic food from this area, not just the big restaurants. And finally â€” do you know if taxis or autos are easily available at this time of day, or should I book one online? Thank you so much â€” I really appreciate your help!

content.instruction: Think about a time you needed help in an unfamiliar place â€” a new city, hospital, government office, airport. How did you ask for help? Recount the experience using indirect questions.
content.prompt: Describe a real or imagined experience where you used or should have used indirect questions to ask for information politely.
content.example_response: When I first moved to Bangalore from my hometown, everything was new and a little overwhelming. I had to visit the Regional Passport Office for the first time and I was very nervous. I didn't know the process at all. When I arrived, I approached the security officer and I said: 'Excuse me, could you tell me where the token counter is?' He directed me inside. Then I asked the person at the counter: 'I was wondering whether I need to submit all original documents today or whether photocopies are sufficient?' She said originals were needed for verification only. I then asked her: 'Do you know approximately how long the process takes for a first-time applicant?' She said around two to three hours. Later I had to ask: 'Could you tell me if there's a place to have lunch nearby?' because I hadn't eaten. Using indirect questions made me feel more confident â€” people responded very warmly when I asked politely.

key_points: Indirect questions are MORE POLITE â€” use with strangers, in formal situations
Common openers: Could you tell meâ€¦? / Do you knowâ€¦? / I was wonderingâ€¦?
WH- questions â†’ use same wh- word + statement word order

lessons.title: Polite requests â€” Would you mind / Could you possibly
lessons.description: Make polite requests in formal and everyday situations using a range of structures from gentle to very polite.

content.title: A scale of politeness for requests
content.body:
Requests in English range from casual to very formal. Choose based on the situation:

MOST CASUAL (friends, family):
â€¢ 'Give me that pen.' / 'Pass the salt.'
â€¢ 'Can you help me?'

POLITE (colleagues, shopkeepers, most situations):
â€¢ 'Could you help me, please?'
â€¢ 'Would you help me?'

MORE POLITE (strangers, bosses, formal):
â€¢ 'Could you possibly help me?'
â€¢ 'Would it be possible to...?'

MOST POLITE (formal or when asking a big favour):
â€¢ 'Would you mind helping me?'
â€¢ 'I was wondering if you could possibly...'
â€¢ 'Would you be so kind as to...?'
content.grammar_note:
COULD = more polite than CAN
WOULD YOU MIND + -ING (gerund)
'Possibly' makes any request softer

content.title: Would you mindâ€¦? â€” special structure
content.body:
WOULD YOU MIND is followed by a GERUND (-ing form):

â€¢ 'Would you mind opening the window?'
â€¢ 'Would you mind waiting for a moment?'
â€¢ 'Would you mind repeating that?'
â€¢ 'Would you mind sending me the document?'

Answering 'Would you mind...?':
âœ“ 'Not at all!' (= I don't mind = Yes, I'll do it)
âœ“ 'Of course!' (= happy to)
âœ“ 'No problem.'
âœ“ 'Sure!'
âœ— 'No.' (this sounds like refusal â€” confusing!)

Note: 'No, not at all' = I'm happy to help (confusing but common)
content.grammar_note:
WOULD YOU MIND + verb-ING
'Not at all' = positive response (I'll do it)
Don't say just 'No' â€” it sounds like refusal

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 3: Customer service call â€” Priya calls a bank, Chennai
content.setting: Priya's debit card was blocked and she needs to get it unblocked. She calls her bank's customer service line. This dialogue features polite requests throughout.
content.learning_note: Notice how BOTH the customer and the bank agent use polite language. This scenario is revisited in Lesson 13 for speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Good afternoon, City Bank customer service. How may I assist you today?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I'm calling because my debit card seems to have been blocked. I'm not sure why.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: I'm sorry to hear that. Could you please verify your name and registered mobile number?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course â€” my name is Priya Rajan and the number is 98765 43210.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Thank you. And could you tell me your account number or the last 4 digits of your card?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: The last 4 digits are 7824.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Perfect. Would you mind holding for a moment while I pull up your account?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Not at all, please go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Thank you for holding, Ms Rajan. I can see your card was blocked after 3 incorrect PIN attempts. Is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh â€” yes, I think I typed the wrong PIN at the petrol station. Could you possibly unblock it for me?

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Absolutely. Would you mind confirming your date of birth for security purposes?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes â€” 14th March 1992.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Perfect. Your card has been unblocked now. Could I ask you to try a transaction and confirm it works?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I will â€” thank you so much! One more thing â€” would it be possible to also increase my daily transaction limit?

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Of course â€” I can raise it up to â‚¹1 lakh per day. Would that work for you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That would be perfect. I really appreciate your help today.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: My pleasure, Ms Rajan. Is there anything else I can help you with?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: No, that's everything. Thank you very much! Bye!

content.instruction: Find and correct the polite request mistake.

sentences.wrong: Would you mind to send the report by tomorrow?
sentences.correct: Would you mind sending the report by tomorrow?
sentences.explanation: WOULD YOU MIND + gerund (-ing). Not 'to send' â€” 'sending'.

sentences.wrong: Could you possibly to help me with this?
sentences.correct: Could you possibly help me with this?
sentences.explanation: After COULD YOU, use base verb directly â€” no 'to'. 'Could you possibly help' not 'to help'.

sentences.wrong: Would it be possible changing the time?
sentences.correct: Would it be possible to change the time?
sentences.explanation: WOULD IT BE POSSIBLE + TO + base verb (infinitive). Not the -ing form.

content.scenario: You are in a library. Someone near you is talking loudly on the phone. It's disturbing you and others. What do you say?
options: Excuse me â€” would you mind taking your call outside? People are trying to study.
content.correct: Excuse me â€” would you mind taking your call outside? People are trying to study.
content.explanation: Public place + stranger = polite request needed. 'Excuse me' softens it further. WOULD YOU MIND + -ing is ideal.

content.instruction: Make 5 polite requests for different situations using different structures from this lesson.
content.prompt:
Create a polite request for each situation:
1. Asking a colleague to cover your work while you're on leave.
2. Asking a stranger to take your photo at a tourist spot.
3. Asking your manager for a one-day deadline extension.
4. Asking a fellow passenger to switch seats on a train.
5. Asking a customer service agent to repeat information slowly.
content.example_response: Here are my five polite requests! For the first one â€” asking a colleague to cover my work: 'Hey Ravi, I was wondering if you could possibly cover my tasks on Thursday while I'm on leave? I'll prepare a detailed handover note so everything is clear.' For the second â€” asking a stranger for a photo: 'Excuse me, would you mind taking a quick photo of us? We're trying to get the whole monument in the frame!' For the manager â€” deadline extension: 'I was wondering if it would be possible to have one additional day for the report? The data analysis is taking longer than I anticipated.' For the train seat â€” 'Excuse me, would you mind swapping seats with me? I'd prefer to sit near the window for the journey.' And for customer service: 'Could you possibly repeat that information a little more slowly? I want to make sure I note it correctly.'
key_phrases: I was wondering if you couldâ€¦

content.instruction: Re-read Scenario 3 (Bank call dialogue). Then role-play: you are calling to dispute a transaction on your credit card. Use polite requests throughout.
content.prompt: Role-play a bank call in which you report an unknown charge on your card. Use: Could you pleaseâ€¦?, Would you mindâ€¦?, I was wondering ifâ€¦, Would it be possible toâ€¦?
content.example_response: Good morning! I'm calling to report an issue with my credit card. I noticed an unfamiliar transaction on my statement and I'd like to get some help with it. Could you please pull up my account? My name is Arjun Mehta and my card number ends in 4391. Yes, thank you for confirming. I was wondering if you could tell me more about a transaction of â‚¹3,200 from a merchant called 'QuickShop Online' dated the 12th? I don't recall making that purchase. It does look suspicious â€” would it be possible to raise a dispute for that transaction? I'd appreciate that. And would you mind sending me the dispute reference number by SMS once it's raised? I'd like to keep a record. Also â€” could you possibly block that card and issue me a replacement? I'm worried there may have been unauthorised access. How long would a replacement take to arrive? Three to five business days â€” that's fine. Thank you so much for your help today!

content.instruction: Complete this email using polite request language.

Subject: Request for Support â€” Project Documentation

Dear Sonal,

I hope you're well. I'm working on the year-end project documentation and I ___ (1) wondering if you ___ (2) possibly share the Q3 reports with me. I understand you may be busy â€” ___ (3) it be possible to send them by Wednesday?

Also, ___ (4) you mind reviewing the summary I've drafted? I'd really ___ (5) a second opinion before I submit it to the manager.

Finally, ___ (6) you possibly spare 20 minutes this week for a quick catch-up call?

content.title: Lesson 4 complete â€” Polite requests
key_points: Scale: Can you? â†’ Could you? â†’ Could you possibly? â†’ Would you mind? â†’ I was wondering if...
WOULD YOU MIND + verb-ING (not 'to')
WOULD IT BE POSSIBLE + TO + base verb
'Not at all' / 'Of course' = positive response to 'Would you mind?'
COULD YOU POSSIBLY = extra polite, good for formal situations
Add 'when you have a moment' / 'if it's not too much trouble' to soften further
content.next_lesson: Phone English â€” calling, holding, transferring

lessons.title: Phone English â€” calling, holding, transferring
lessons.description: Handle phone calls confidently â€” introducing yourself, asking to hold, transferring, leaving and taking messages.

content.title: Starting and ending a phone call
content.body:
STARTING a call â€” caller:
â€¢ 'Good morning, this is [name] calling from [company].'
â€¢ 'Hello, could I speak to [name], please?'
â€¢ 'Hi, I'm calling about [reason].'
â€¢ 'I'm calling to enquire about...'

STARTING â€” receiver:
â€¢ 'Good morning, [company name]. How may I help you?'
â€¢ '[Name] speaking â€” how can I help?'
â€¢ 'Hello, who am I speaking to?'

ENDING a call:
â€¢ 'Thank you for calling. Have a good day!'
â€¢ 'I'll look into that and get back to you.'
â€¢ 'Thanks â€” speak soon!'
â€¢ 'Goodbye!'
content.grammar_note:
'This is [name]' â€” NOT 'I am [name]' on phone calls
'Could I speak to...' â€” more polite than 'Give me'
Receiver: '[Name] speaking' = professional

content.title: Holding, transferring and taking messages
content.body:
ASKING TO HOLD:
â€¢ 'Could you hold the line, please?'
â€¢ 'Could you hold on a moment?'
â€¢ 'Please bear with me a moment.'
â€¢ 'Just a moment, please.'

TRANSFERRING:
â€¢ 'I'll transfer you to the right department.'
â€¢ 'Let me put you through to Ms Priya.'
â€¢ 'I'll connect you to the sales team.'

TAKING A MESSAGE:
â€¢ 'She's not available â€” can I take a message?'
â€¢ 'Could I take your name and number?'
â€¢ 'I'll make sure she gets this message.'

LEAVING A MESSAGE:
â€¢ 'Yes â€” could you tell her that [name] called?'
â€¢ 'Could you ask her to call me back?'
â€¢ 'My number is...'
content.grammar_note:
'Put you through' = transfer the call
'Bear with me' = please wait patiently
'I'll get back to you' = I'll call/email you later

lessons.title: Social media & texting language
lessons.description: Understand common digital communication styles â€” abbreviations, emoji use, and the difference between formal and informal digital writing.

content.title: Texting and social media language
content.body:
In digital communication, people often use SHORTENED LANGUAGE â€” especially in casual messages:

COMMON ABBREVIATIONS:
â€¢ btw = by the way
â€¢ lol = laughing out loud
â€¢ omg = oh my goodness
â€¢ tbh = to be honest
â€¢ imo = in my opinion
â€¢ fyi = for your information
â€¢ asap = as soon as possible
â€¢ brb = be right back
â€¢ ngl = not gonna lie
â€¢ icymi = in case you missed it

IMPORTANT: These are ONLY for casual, informal messages to friends. Never use them in formal emails, professional messages, or with seniors/strangers.
content.grammar_note:
Abbreviations = informal only
Capitalisation often dropped in texts
Emoji use = casual contexts only

content.title: Online etiquette â€” dos and don'ts
content.body:
GOOD digital communication:
âœ“ Reply promptly when possible
âœ“ Use full words in professional messages
âœ“ Double-check before sending
âœ“ Match the tone of the person you're writing to
âœ“ Use emoji sparingly and appropriately
âœ“ Be clear â€” avoid confusion

AVOID:
âœ— All caps (sounds like shouting)
âœ— Sending too many follow-up messages
âœ— Using abbreviations in professional contexts
âœ— Sharing personal/private information publicly
âœ— Responding emotionally when angry â€” wait before replying
âœ— Reading messages and not replying for days

A good rule: Would I say this to their face? If not, reconsider.
content.grammar_note:
ALL CAPS = shouting in digital communication
Professional messages: full words, no abbreviations
Read, pause, then reply if emotional

content.instruction: Compare two types of messages â€” informal (to a friend) and formal (to a manager) â€” for the same situation: you need to take a day off tomorrow.
content.prompt: Write and say two messages â€” one casual to a friend, one professional to your manager â€” about taking a day off tomorrow.
content.example_response:
Here are both versions!

Casual WhatsApp to my friend Meera: 'Hey! Ngl I'm totally exhausted ðŸ˜… I'm taking tomorrow off! Btw can we do our walk on Saturday instead? Omg I cannot wait to just sleep in lol ðŸ˜‚'

Professional message to my manager: 'Hi [Manager's name] â€” I wanted to let you know that I'm not feeling well and would like to take a sick day tomorrow if that's okay. I'll make sure all urgent tasks are completed or handed over before I log off today. I'll share a brief update on pending work by 5 PM. Please let me know if this causes any issues. Thank you for understanding.'

You can see the difference clearly â€” with my friend I use abbreviations, emoji, and very casual phrasing. With my manager, I explain the situation clearly, offer to manage my work responsibly, and ask for permission politely. Same situation, completely different language â€” and that's exactly how it should be.
key_phrases: formal: I wanted to let you know, I'll ensure, Thank you for understanding

content.instruction: Think about how social media and digital communication have changed the way people communicate in India. What are the positive changes? What are the problems? Share your opinion.
content.prompt: Discuss the impact of social media and digital communication on everyday life in India â€” pros and cons.
content.example_response: Social media and digital communication have completely transformed how people communicate in India â€” and there are both good and not-so-good sides to this. On the positive side, staying connected with family members who live far away has become so easy. My cousins are spread across Chennai, Bangalore, Hyderabad, and even the UK â€” and we all talk on a family WhatsApp group every day. Without that, we'd barely know what was happening in each other's lives. Digital communication has also made business much faster â€” everything from job applications to client meetings can happen online now. I think that's genuinely wonderful. However, there are problems too. A lot of people â€” especially young people â€” spend far too much time on social media and it affects their mental health. They compare their lives to others and feel inadequate. There's also the problem of misinformation spreading very quickly through WhatsApp forwards. And tbh, sometimes I think people have forgotten how to have a proper face-to-face conversation â€” everyone's on their phone even when they're together. It's a bit sad. So overall â€” a brilliant tool, but we need to use it wisely.

lessons.title: Online communication â€” professional chat and email
lessons.description: Write and respond to professional messages, team chats, and work emails with the right tone and structure.

content.title: Semi-formal professional chat language
content.body:
Most modern workplaces use chat tools (Slack, Teams, WhatsApp Work). This is SEMI-FORMAL â€” friendly but professional.

Good semi-formal chat:
â€¢ 'Hi Priya â€” could you send me the report when you get a chance?'
â€¢ 'Hey team â€” quick update: the call is at 3, not 2.'
â€¢ 'Thanks for the heads up!'
â€¢ 'I'll look into this and get back to you shortly.'
â€¢ 'Happy to help â€” just send it over.'
â€¢ 'Noted â€” I'll take care of it.'

Avoid in work chats:
â€¢ 'omg thx lol'
â€¢ 'K' (too curt â€” sounds rude)
â€¢ All caps
â€¢ Ignoring messages
content.grammar_note:
Semi-formal = friendly + professional
'Heads up' = advance notice (informal but acceptable in chats)
'Noted' = I understand and will act on it

content.title: Useful professional chat phrases
content.body:
GIVING UPDATES:
â€¢ 'Just to let you know...'
â€¢ 'Quick update â€” the meeting is confirmed for 3 PM.'
â€¢ 'FYI â€” the client has pushed back the deadline.'

ACKNOWLEDGING:
â€¢ 'Got it â€” thanks!'
â€¢ 'Noted â€” I'll action this today.'
â€¢ 'Understood â€” I'll update the team.'

ASKING FOR HELP:
â€¢ 'Could you take a look at this when you're free?'
â€¢ 'Happy for any feedback on this.'
â€¢ 'Would you be able to cover this while I'm away?'

SHARING DOCUMENTS:
â€¢ 'Sharing the updated file here.'
â€¢ 'See the attached â€” let me know if anything needs changing.'
â€¢ 'I've dropped the doc in the shared folder.'
content.grammar_note:
'Action this' = to take action on something (professional verb)
'Happy for feedback' = I welcome feedback
'Drop a doc' = informal way of sharing a file

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 5: Workplace team chat â€” marketing startup, Mumbai

content.instruction:
Revisit Scenario 5 â€” the team chat dialogue. Answer these questions in detail:
1. How does the team handle the sudden schedule change?
2. What does Meera's message add to the conversation?
3. What professional chat habits does this team show?
content.prompt: Analyse the team chat dialogue and describe what makes it effective professional communication.
content.example_response: The team in Scenario 5 handles the sudden schedule change very efficiently. Priya, the team lead, immediately sends a clear heads-up message â€” she states what changed, when, and what everyone needs to do â€” update their calendars. There's no panic, no confusion. Arjun responds immediately and proactively asks whether he needs to do anything additional â€” that shows initiative and professionalism. Meera's question adds real value to the conversation. She raises an important point that nobody else had thought of â€” whether the client had already seen the proposal. This is what Priya calls a 'good flag'. It shows that Meera is thinking beyond just attending the meeting. In terms of professional chat habits, the team shows several excellent practices. They acknowledge messages quickly â€” 'Got it', 'Noted', 'Will do'. They give specific timelines â€” 'draft by Wednesday evening', 'by today EOD'. They stay solution-focused rather than complaining about the change. And they're friendly â€” a friendly emoji or two is perfectly fine in a team chat. The result is a chat that is efficient, clear, and pleasant to be part of.

key_points: Semi-formal = friendly + professional â€” right tone for most workplace chats
Key phrases: 'Just a heads up', 'Noted', 'Got it', 'I'll action this', 'Loop in', 'Flag'
Always acknowledge messages â€” even 'Got it â€” thanks' is better than no reply

Avoid: single letters ('K'), all caps, too many question marks, ignoring messages

lessons.title: Small talk â€” topics and reactions
lessons.description: Start and sustain small talk conversations about common topics â€” weather, sport, food, weekend plans, and current events.

content.title: What is small talk and why does it matter?
content.body:
SMALL TALK is light, casual conversation about everyday topics. It's important because:
â€¢ It builds relationships
â€¢ It breaks the ice with new people
â€¢ It fills comfortable silences
â€¢ It's the first step to deeper conversations

COMMON SMALL TALK TOPICS:
âœ“ Weather: 'Can you believe this heat?'
âœ“ Weekend plans: 'Any plans this weekend?'
âœ“ Food: 'Have you tried the new place on the corner?'
âœ“ Work (general): 'How's work going?'
âœ“ Sport: 'Did you watch the match last night?'
âœ“ Travel: 'Have you been anywhere nice recently?'
âœ“ Current events (positive/neutral): 'Did you see the festival in the city?'

AVOID for first small talk:
âœ— Money, salary, politics (controversial)
âœ— Personal family problems
âœ— Negative complaints about people
content.grammar_note:
Small talk uses present simple, present perfect (Have you...?), and past simple
Questions are open-ended to encourage the other person to talk

content.title: Reaction phrases and keeping conversation going
content.body:
Show you're listening and engaged:

REACTIONS:
â€¢ Really? / Oh wow!
â€¢ That sounds lovely / That sounds tough!
â€¢ How interesting!
â€¢ You're kidding!
â€¢ Tell me more!
â€¢ I can imagine.
â€¢ Oh no! / That's great!

FOLLOW-UP QUESTIONS to keep talking:
â€¢ 'What was it like?'
â€¢ 'How long were you there?'
â€¢ 'Did you enjoy it?'
â€¢ 'Would you recommend it?'
â€¢ 'What do you think of it?'
â€¢ 'Was it your first time?'
content.grammar_note:
Reactions show you're actively listening
Follow-up questions are WH- or YES/NO questions
Avoid one-word answers in small talk

content.instruction: Practise starting small talk in 3 different situations. For each one, give an opening sentence AND a follow-up question after the person responds.
content.prompt: Start small talk in: (1) a company lift on a Monday, (2) while waiting for a delayed train, (3) at a friend's birthday party with new people.
content.example_response:
Situation 1 â€” Lift on a Monday morning: 'Morning! Can you believe the traffic today? I was stuck near the signal for almost 40 minutes! Is your commute usually this bad?' If they say yes: 'Oh no â€” which route do you take? I should probably try the metro more often honestly!'

Situation 2 â€” Delayed train: 'This delay is something else, isn't it? I've been here 30 minutes already. Are you travelling far? At least the platform isn't too crowded!' If they say they're going to Coimbatore: 'Oh lovely â€” is it a holiday trip or visiting family?'

Situation 3 â€” Birthday party: 'Great party, isn't it? The food is amazing â€” have you tried that mango dessert? I think I've had three already! Are you a friend of Meera's from work or college?' If they say college: 'Oh nice! Which college? I was in Bangalore for three years myself â€” whereabouts?'

content.instruction: Think about the best small talk conversation you've ever had â€” or imagine one. What was the topic? How did it start? Where did it go? What made it so enjoyable?
content.prompt: Describe a memorable small talk conversation â€” real or imagined â€” that flowed really well.
content.example_response: One of the best small talk conversations I've ever had was completely unexpected. I was waiting at a bus stop in Chennai during a sudden downpour â€” absolutely soaked and a bit annoyed. An elderly gentleman next to me said, 'Well, this rain has no manners, does it?' â€” which made me laugh immediately. We started talking about the monsoon and how unpredictable the Chennai rains are. He told me he'd lived in the city for over 60 years and could predict rain by looking at the sea. I was fascinated. It led to a conversation about how the city had changed â€” the old Madras versus new Chennai. He knew so many beautiful stories about the old neighbourhood markets, the old cinema halls, the filter coffee culture. By the time the bus came, almost 20 minutes had passed and I was genuinely sad to end the conversation. What made it special was that it started with something simple â€” the weather â€” and turned into this rich, unexpected exchange. The best small talk always does that.

content.title: Lesson 8 complete â€” Small talk
key_points: Safe topics: weather, food, weekend, travel, sport, festivals â€” NOT salary, politics, or personal problems
Open with relatable openers: 'Can you believeâ€¦?', 'Have you triedâ€¦?', 'Any plans forâ€¦?'
React with interest: 'Oh wow!', 'Really?', 'That sounds amazing!', 'You're kidding!'
Follow-up questions keep the conversation alive: 'How was it?', 'Would you recommend it?'
Return the question: after answering, ask back â€” 'What about you?'
Never give one-word answers in small talk â€” always add something

lessons.title: Reactions and follow-up questions
lessons.description: Respond naturally to what people say â€” express surprise, sympathy, happiness â€” and ask the right questions to keep conversation going.

content.title: Reacting naturally to news
content.body:
Different types of news need different reactions:

GOOD NEWS:
â€¢ 'That's wonderful news!'
â€¢ 'Congratulations!'
â€¢ 'I'm so happy for you!'
â€¢ 'You must be thrilled!'
â€¢ 'Oh wow â€” that's brilliant!'

BAD NEWS:
â€¢ 'Oh no â€” I'm so sorry to hear that.'
â€¢ 'That's terrible â€” are you okay?'
â€¢ 'I can imagine how hard that must be.'
â€¢ 'Please let me know if there's anything I can do.'

SURPRISING NEWS:
â€¢ 'Really? I had no idea!'
â€¢ 'Seriously? I can't believe it!'
â€¢ 'You're joking! / No way!'
â€¢ 'That's incredible!'

INTERESTING NEWS:
â€¢ 'How fascinating!'
â€¢ 'That's really interesting â€” tell me more!'
â€¢ 'I didn't know that!'
content.grammar_note:
Reactions use exclamations and short phrases
'Must be' is used to empathise: 'You must be so excited!'
'I can imagine' shows you understand their feelings

content.title: Echoing and showing active listening
content.body:
ECHOING = repeating part of what someone said to show you're listening:

â€¢ 'I got the promotion!' â†’ 'The promotion! That's wonderful!'
â€¢ 'We moved to a new flat.' â†’ 'A new flat â€” how exciting!'
â€¢ 'I failed the exam.' â†’ 'Failed? Oh no â€” I'm so sorry!'

Other active listening techniques:
â€¢ Mm-hmm / Right / I see (shows you're following)
â€¢ 'Oh really?' (surprise + invitation to continue)
â€¢ 'And then what happened?' (asking for more detail)
â€¢ 'How did that make you feel?' (empathy)
â€¢ 'I bet!' (I imagine that!)
â€¢ 'That said a lot.' / 'That must have taken courage.'
content.grammar_note:
Echoing = repeat a KEY WORD with an exclamation
Active listening = show you're engaged, not just waiting to speak
'I bet!' = I can well imagine!

content.title: Catching up after a while â€” college friends, Bangalore coffee shop
content.setting: Priya (from Chennai) is visiting Bangalore and meets her old friend Kavya. They haven't spoken in months. The conversation is full of natural reactions and follow-up questions.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Priya! Oh my goodness â€” you look amazing. Sit, sit! How have you been?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I've been good! A bit all over the place honestly. So much has happened since we last talked!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Tell me everything! What's been going on?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Okay â€” so the big news: I resigned from my job three months ago.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: What?! You resigned? I had no idea â€” are you okay? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I'm actually great! It was tough at first but the job was making me miserable for a long time.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I can imagine â€” you seemed quite stressed whenever we spoke last year. So what are you doing now?

dialogue.text: It took about a month to get the first client, then it kind of snowballed. But the big thing is â€” I'm actually happy at work for the first time in years!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That's such wonderful news, Priya â€” you must be so relieved! I'm genuinely happy for you.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I am! And what about you? You look well â€” anything new on your end?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Well... I got engaged last month!

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: No way! Kavya! Congratulations â€” I'm so happy for you! Who is he? Tell me everything!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! His name is Arjun â€” he works in Bangalore, we've been together for two years. It happened quite suddenly but it felt right.

dialogue.speaker: Priya

content.instruction: These reactions are inappropriate or unnatural. Correct them.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'I got promoted!' You: 'I knew I was better than you for that role.'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'I got promoted!' You: 'That's amazing news â€” you really deserved it! Congratulations!'
sentences.explanation: Never make someone's good news about you or compare. Celebrate genuinely.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'My grandmother passed away.' You: 'Oh well, she was very old, wasn't she?'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'My grandmother passed away.' You: 'I'm so sorry to hear that â€” she sounded like such a wonderful person. Are you doing okay?'
sentences.explanation: Never minimise someone's grief. Show empathy and ask how they're coping.

sentences.wrong: Friend: 'I'm moving abroad!' You: 'Don't you think that's a bit risky?'
sentences.correct: Friend: 'I'm moving abroad!' You: 'Wow â€” that's such an exciting step! Where are you going? You must be so excited!'
sentences.explanation: Don't question their decision with negativity â€” react positively and show interest.

content.scenario: Your colleague comes to work looking very upset. When you ask, she says she didn't get the project she applied for. She was really hoping for it. What do you say?
options: That's okay, there will be other projects.
Oh, I'm really sorry â€” you must be so disappointed. You worked so hard for that. Is there anything I can do?
content.correct: Oh, I'm really sorry â€” you must be so disappointed. You worked so hard for that. Is there anything I can do?
content.explanation: Acknowledge the feeling ('must be disappointed'), validate the effort ('worked so hard'), and offer support.

content.instruction: Respond to these 5 pieces of news with a natural reaction + follow-up question. Speak for at least 2 sentences per response.
content.prompt:
React to each piece of news:
1. 'I'm going to become a vegetarian!'
2. 'My flight was diverted to the wrong city.'
3. 'I finished learning Japanese in one year.'
4. 'My company offered me a transfer to London.'
5. 'I've decided to run a half marathon next month.'
content.example_response:
1. 'I'm going to become a vegetarian!' â€” Oh wow, that's a big change! What inspired the decision? Are you going full vegetarian or starting gradually? I think that's great â€” I've thought about it too sometimes.

2. 'My flight was diverted to the wrong city.' â€” Oh no, you're joking! That sounds like an absolute nightmare! Where did you end up? How long did it take to sort out? You must have been exhausted!

3. 'I finished learning Japanese in one year.' â€” Are you serious?! That's incredible â€” Japanese is supposed to be one of the hardest languages in the world! How did you do it? Were you taking formal classes?

4. 'My company offered me a transfer to London.' â€” Oh wow â€” that's huge! Are you excited or nervous? Have you decided whether you're going to accept? London is amazing but it's a big move!

5. 'I've decided to run a half marathon next month.' â€” Really?! That's so impressive â€” have you been training for a while? A half marathon is no joke â€” 21 kilometres! I bet you'll do brilliantly!

content.instruction: Share a piece of news from your own life â€” real or imagined â€” and describe how people reacted to it. Were their reactions good? What made a reaction feel warm or cold?
content.prompt: Tell a story about sharing some news with someone and how they reacted. What did they say? How did it make you feel?
content.example_response: I'd like to talk about when I told my family I'd decided to leave my stable engineering job to try freelancing. I was very nervous because I knew it was an unconventional choice. When I told my mother, her first reaction was quiet â€” then she said: 'Are you sure? What if it doesn't work out? You have a good job.' And honestly, even though she meant well, that reaction made me feel deflated. I'd expected more support. Then I told my best friend and she said: 'Oh my god â€” that's so brave! You've been wanting to do this for years. I'm so proud of you. What are your plans? How can I help?' And that reaction changed everything. I felt validated and excited. The same news got two completely different reactions â€” one focused on the risk, one on the possibility. I learned that when someone shares big news, the most important thing is to lead with warmth and interest, not with fear. Ask questions, show excitement, and save the practical concerns for later.

Kavya: March! We're both over the moon.

Kavya: A bit tired but really happy. Oh â€” and my sister failed her board exams.

Priya: Oh ___ (4) â€” ___ (5) be so disappointed. Is she doing okay?

Kavya: She's upset but she's planning to retake them in six months.

Priya: That's the spirit â€” she'll do brilliantly next time! And your job â€” still loving it?

Kavya: Actually, I got a pay rise last month!

Priya: ___ (6)?! That's ___ (7) news â€” you completely ___ (8) it!

content.title: Lesson 9 complete â€” Reactions and follow-up questions
key_points: Good news: 'That's wonderful!', 'Congratulations!', 'You must be thrilled!'
Bad news: 'I'm so sorry', 'That sounds so tough', 'I can imagine'
Surprise: 'Really?!', 'You're joking!', 'No way!'
Echoing = repeat a key word: 'A promotion? That's amazing!'
Active listening: Mm-hmm, Right, I see, I bet!
Follow-up questions: 'How longâ€¦?', 'Was it your first time?', 'How did that feel?'
Never minimise someone's news â€” always lead with warmth

lessons.title: Expressing opinions and agreeing/disagreeing
lessons.description: Share your views confidently, agree and disagree politely, and discuss everyday topics using natural opinion language.

content.title: Expressing opinions â€” a full toolkit
content.body:
There are many ways to share your opinion in English. Use stronger or softer language depending on how certain you are:

STRONG opinion:
â€¢ 'I strongly believe that...'
â€¢ 'I'm absolutely convinced that...'
â€¢ 'There's no doubt in my mind that...'

MILD opinion:
â€¢ 'I think...'
â€¢ 'I feel that...'
â€¢ 'In my opinion,...'
â€¢ 'Personally, I...'
â€¢ 'As far as I'm concerned,...'
â€¢ 'From my perspective,...'

SOFT / HEDGED opinion:
â€¢ 'I suppose...'
â€¢ 'I tend to think...'
â€¢ 'I'm inclined to think...'
â€¢ 'It seems to me that...'
content.grammar_note:
Opinion phrases come BEFORE the main statement
'I think + clause' â€” no 'to' between
'In my opinion' = formal; 'If you ask me' = informal

content.title: Agreeing and disagreeing politely
content.body:
AGREEING:
â€¢ 'Absolutely!'
â€¢ 'Exactly â€” that's what I think too.'
â€¢ 'I completely agree.'
â€¢ 'You're absolutely right.'
â€¢ 'That's a very good point.'
â€¢ 'I couldn't agree more.'
â€¢ 'That's exactly how I feel.'

PARTIAL AGREEMENT:
â€¢ 'You have a point, but...'
â€¢ 'I agree up to a point, however...'
â€¢ 'That's true to some extent, but...'
â€¢ 'I see where you're coming from, although...'

DISAGREEING POLITELY:
â€¢ 'I'm not sure I agree with that.'
â€¢ 'I see it slightly differently.'
â€¢ 'I take your point, but...'
â€¢ 'With respect, I think...'
â€¢ 'I'm afraid I disagree â€” in my view...'
content.grammar_note:
Partial agreement = acknowledge + BUT
Always soften disagreement â€” never just say 'Wrong!'
'With respect' = polite way to introduce disagreement

content.title: Vocabulary: Discussion and debate language

vocabulary.meaning: a particular way of looking at something
vocabulary.example: From my perspective, remote work has more benefits.

vocabulary.meaning: having a good, solid basis
vocabulary.example: That's a valid point â€” I hadn't thought of it that way.

vocabulary.meaning: causing strong disagreement
vocabulary.example: This is a controversial topic with many different views.

vocabulary.meaning: a reason given to support an opinion
vocabulary.example: Her main argument was that quality matters more than quantity.

vocabulary.meaning: an opposing argument
vocabulary.example: I understand your view, but here's the counterpoint.

vocabulary.meaning: taking into account different sides and shades of meaning
vocabulary.example: It's a nuanced issue â€” neither side is completely right.

sentences: In my opinion, working from home is more productive for most people.
I completely agree â€” the flexibility really helps focus.
I see your point, but I think office collaboration is also very important.
That's a valid point â€” I suppose it depends on the type of work.
I'm inclined to think that a hybrid model works best for everyone.

content.question: Which phrase best introduces a STRONG personal opinion?
options: I strongly believe that quality education should be free for everyone.
content.correct: I strongly believe that quality education should be free for everyone.
content.explanation: 'I strongly believe' = clear, confident, personal opinion. The others are hedged or distanced.

content.question: Someone shares an opinion you partly agree with but have a counterpoint to. Which response is best?
options: I see where you're coming from, although I think there's another side to consider.
That is not a good point.
content.correct: I see where you're coming from, although I think there's another side to consider.
content.explanation: Partial agreement: acknowledge their view ('I see where you're coming from') + add your counterpoint ('although...').

content.question: Which phrase is the most polite way to DISAGREE?
options: I'm afraid I see it slightly differently â€” in my view...
content.correct: I'm afraid I see it slightly differently â€” in my view...
content.explanation: 'I'm afraid' softens; 'see it differently' disagrees without attacking. Then state your own view calmly.

content.question: What does 'I couldn't agree more' mean?
options: I agree with some parts only.
content.correct: I completely and strongly agree.
content.explanation: 'I couldn't agree more' = maximum agreement. The double negative ('couldn't' + 'more') creates emphasis.

content.title: Opinion debate â€” work from home vs office â€” Mumbai startup
content.setting: Three colleagues â€” Priya, Arjun, and Meera â€” are having a friendly debate during lunch about remote working. They all have different views.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I strongly believe that working from home is the future. The productivity stats speak for themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I completely agree about productivity for individual tasks. But I think collaboration suffers when everyone is remote.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I see your point, but tools like Slack and Teams have made collaboration much easier. Physical presence isn't always necessary.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: I'm inclined to think a hybrid model is the answer. In my opinion, neither extreme is ideal.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: That's a valid point, Meera. I agree up to a point â€” but I think junior employees especially need to be in the office to learn and grow.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I take your point on that. There's definitely a difference between senior staff and people just starting out.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Exactly! From my perspective, the best arrangement depends on the role, the team, and the individual.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I couldn't agree more with that last point. It's not one-size-fits-all.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Personally, what I find most valuable about remote work is the time saved on commuting. Two hours a day back in my life is enormous.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.text: Oh, I absolutely agree there â€” the commute factor alone is life-changing for many people in Mumbai.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Fair enough â€” I'm coming around to the idea. I suppose what I really want is choice. The freedom to decide for myself.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Now that's something I think all three of us can agree on!

What does Meera say is the best working arrangement?

content.instruction: Find and correct the opinion language mistake.

sentences.wrong: In my opinion I think that technology is good.
sentences.correct: In my opinion, technology has been largely beneficial. / I think technology is generally good.
sentences.explanation: 'In my opinion' and 'I think' mean the same â€” don't use both together. Choose one.

sentences.wrong: I see your point but you are wrong.
sentences.correct: I see your point, but I think there's another perspective worth considering.
sentences.explanation: After acknowledging their point, disagree constructively â€” not bluntly. Explain your view instead.

sentences.wrong: I couldn't agree less with you completely.
sentences.correct: I couldn't agree more. / I completely agree.
sentences.explanation: 'I couldn't agree more' = maximum agreement. 'I couldn't agree less' = maximum disagreement. These mean opposite things â€” don't mix them.

content.scenario: In a team meeting, a senior colleague presents an idea you think has a flaw. How do you politely raise your concern?
options: That's a really interesting approach. I wonder if we've also considered what might happen if the budget runs over â€” could that be a challenge?
content.correct: That's a really interesting approach. I wonder if we've also considered what might happen if the budget runs over â€” could that be a challenge?
content.explanation: Acknowledge the idea positively + raise concern as a question = professional and constructive disagreement.

content.instruction: Give your opinion on this topic for 90 seconds: 'Social media does more harm than good.' Express your view clearly, acknowledge the other side, and explain your reasoning.
content.prompt: Express your opinion on social media being harmful or beneficial. Use strong opinion language AND partial agreement/counterpoints.
content.example_response: In my opinion, social media does more harm than good â€” but I'll acknowledge that it's a nuanced issue. Let me start with the problems. From my perspective, social media has contributed significantly to mental health issues, especially among young people. The constant comparison to other people's highlight reels creates unrealistic expectations and a deep sense of inadequacy. I strongly believe this is one of the biggest challenges of our generation. The spread of misinformation is another serious concern â€” false news travels much faster than facts, and I think that's genuinely dangerous for society. That said, I do see the counterarguments. Social media has made it possible for communities to organise, for small businesses to reach customers they never could before, and for people separated by distance to stay connected. I take that point â€” those are real benefits. But on balance, as far as I'm concerned, the mental health impact and the misinformation crisis outweigh the positives â€” at least the way social media currently works. I'm inclined to think that with better regulation and education, it could be reformed into something much healthier.

content.instruction: Read the debate dialogue again. Then discuss: which speaker â€” Priya, Arjun, or Meera â€” do YOU agree with most, and why?
content.prompt: Evaluate the three positions in the work-from-home debate and explain which you find most convincing.
content.example_response: Having read all three positions in the debate, I find myself agreeing most with Meera's view â€” that a hybrid model is the best solution. And I'll explain why. Priya makes some strong points about productivity and the value of saved commuting time â€” I absolutely see where she's coming from and I think she's right about individual focused work being better at home. However, I take Arjun's point too â€” I think collaboration and mentoring do suffer with full remote work. Junior employees especially need that in-person learning environment. Meera's position, to my mind, is the most thoughtful because it acknowledges both sides rather than taking an extreme stance. She says the right arrangement depends on the role, the team, and the individual â€” and I strongly believe that's true. A data analyst may work perfectly well remotely, while a new junior designer might need office time to absorb the culture and get quick feedback. As far as I'm concerned, the best companies will be those that trust their employees to know what works for them â€” with some structure around team time. So yes â€” I'm very much in Meera's camp on this one!
key_phrases: I absolutely see where they're coming from

That ___ (5), I do think some aspects of the system are strong. The emphasis on mathematics and science, ___ (6) example, has produced some of the world's best engineers and doctors. ___ (7) point, Ravi â€” and it's a fair one.

___ (8) the whole, however, I am ___ (9) to think that a more skills-based, project-driven approach would serve students better in today's world. ___ (10) me that the countries producing the most innovative graduates are those with the most flexible education systems.

content.title: Lesson 10 complete â€” Expressing opinions and agreeing/disagreeing
key_points: Strong: 'I strongly believe / I'm absolutely convinced'
Mild: 'I think / I feel / In my opinion / Personally'
Soft: 'I suppose / I tend to think / It seems to me'
Full agreement: 'I couldn't agree more / Absolutely / Exactly'
Partial agreement: 'You have a point, but... / I agree up to a point, however...'
Polite disagreement: 'I see it differently / I'm afraid I disagree / With respect, I think...'
Don't use 'In my opinion' AND 'I think' together â€” they mean the same

lessons.title: Complaints and requests in person
lessons.description: Make polite complaints and requests face-to-face â€” at hotels, shops, restaurants, and offices â€” and handle responses professionally.

content.title: How to make a polite complaint
content.body:
A good complaint has THREE parts:
1. EXPLAIN the problem clearly
2. GIVE a reason why it matters
3. REQUEST a solution

Useful language:
â€¢ 'I'm afraid there's a problem with...'
â€¢ 'I'd like to bring something to your attention.'
â€¢ 'I'm not entirely happy with...'
â€¢ 'I think there may have been a mistake with...'
â€¢ 'I was expecting... but instead...'
â€¢ 'This isn't quite what I ordered / asked for.'

Requesting a solution:
â€¢ 'Could you possibly...?'
â€¢ 'I'd appreciate it if you could...'
â€¢ 'Would it be possible to...?'
â€¢ 'I'd like a replacement / refund / correction, please.'
content.grammar_note:
Polite complaints use 'I'm afraid' to soften
'I'm not entirely happy' = softer than 'This is terrible'
Always request a solution, not just complain

content.title: Handling a complaint â€” the staff side
content.body:
If you work in service and receive a complaint, respond professionally:

ACKNOWLEDGING:
â€¢ 'I understand completely â€” I'm very sorry about that.'
â€¢ 'Thank you for bringing this to our attention.'
â€¢ 'I sincerely apologise for the inconvenience.'

OFFERING SOLUTIONS:
â€¢ 'I'll look into this right away.'
â€¢ 'I can offer you a replacement / refund / discount.'
â€¢ 'Let me speak to the manager for you.'
â€¢ 'We'll make sure this doesn't happen again.'

NEVER say:
âœ— 'That's not my problem.'
âœ— 'You should have checked before.'
âœ— 'I can't do anything about it.'
content.grammar_note:
Always acknowledge before offering a solution
'I'll look into this' = I will investigate immediately
Never blame the customer

content.title: Vocabulary: Complaints and service

vocabulary.meaning: broken or not working properly
vocabulary.example: The charger I bought is defective â€” it doesn't work.

vocabulary.meaning: made to pay too much
vocabulary.example: I think I've been overcharged â€” the bill seems too high.

vocabulary.meaning: money given back after returning a product
vocabulary.example: I'd like a full refund, please.

vocabulary.meaning: to swap a product for another
vocabulary.example: Could I exchange this for a larger size?

vocabulary.meaning: to take a problem to a higher level
vocabulary.example: If this isn't resolved, I'll need to escalate the complaint.

vocabulary.meaning: the solution to a problem or complaint
vocabulary.example: Thank you â€” I'm happy with this resolution.

sentences: I'm afraid there's a problem with my room â€” the air conditioning isn't working.
I think there may have been a mistake with my bill â€” I was charged for a dish I didn't order.
I understand completely â€” I'll look into this right away.
Could you possibly send someone to fix it as soon as possible?
I'd like to return this â€” it seems to be defective.

content.question: You ordered a vegetarian meal but received a non-vegetarian one. Which is the most polite complaint?
options: This is wrong! Take it back!
Excuse me â€” I'm afraid this isn't what I ordered. I asked for the vegetarian option. Could you check and replace it, please?
content.correct: Excuse me â€” I'm afraid this isn't what I ordered. I asked for the vegetarian option. Could you check and replace it, please?
content.explanation: 'I'm afraid' softens the complaint. State the problem clearly, reference your original request, and politely ask for a solution.

content.question: You bought a pair of headphones and one side doesn't work. What do you say at the shop?
options: These headphones are broken! Give me money back!
Excuse me â€” I bought these headphones yesterday and I'm afraid one side appears to be defective. I'd like to exchange them or get a refund, please.
I want to complain about everything in this shop.
content.correct: Excuse me â€” I bought these headphones yesterday and I'm afraid one side appears to be defective. I'd like to exchange them or get a refund, please.
content.explanation: Polite structure: context (when you bought) + problem ('I'm afraid' + defective) + clear request (exchange or refund).

content.question: A customer complains that their hotel room was not cleaned. The best staff response is:
options: I sincerely apologise for this â€” that's certainly not the standard we aim for. I'll send housekeeping up immediately.
The room was cleaned â€” you must be wrong.
content.correct: I sincerely apologise for this â€” that's certainly not the standard we aim for. I'll send housekeeping up immediately.
content.explanation: Acknowledge, apologise, reassure, and offer immediate action. Never blame the customer or defend poorly.

content.question: You check your restaurant bill and notice you've been charged for a dessert you didn't have. What do you say?
options: Excuse me â€” I think there might be an error on my bill. I see a charge for dessert, but I don't believe we ordered any. Could you double-check?
The bill is wrong. Change it.
content.correct: Excuse me â€” I think there might be an error on my bill. I see a charge for dessert, but I don't believe we ordered any. Could you double-check?
content.explanation: 'I think there might be an error' = polite framing. 'I don't believe' = less accusatory than 'you made a mistake'.

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 4: Hotel complaint and resolution â€” Chennai
content.setting: Kavya checks into a business hotel in Chennai. Several things are not right with her room and she needs to sort them out. This dialogue features polite complaints, professional staff responses, and a successful resolution.
content.learning_note: Notice how Kavya stays calm and specific throughout â€” and how the staff responds with genuine solutions. This scenario is revisited in Lesson 13.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Excuse me â€” I checked in about an hour ago. I'm in Room 412.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Yes of course â€” how can I help you, Ms Iyer?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I'm afraid there are a couple of issues with my room. First â€” the air conditioning isn't cooling properly. It's quite warm in there.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: I sincerely apologise for that. I'll send our maintenance team up right away to check it.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Thank you. The second issue is â€” I requested a non-smoking room when I booked, but the room smells strongly of smoke.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Oh â€” I'm very sorry about that. That's certainly not acceptable. Would you like us to move you to a different room?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, I think that would be best. Could you possibly find me a room on a higher floor? I'd prefer the view as well if that's possible.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Of course â€” let me check availability. We do have Room 608 on the sixth floor â€” it's a deluxe room with a city view. I can move you there at no extra charge.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That sounds perfect. One more thing â€” I was charged for airport pickup in my bill but I actually made my own way here.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: I'll look into that right away. I can see there may have been a mix-up with the booking. I'll remove that charge from your bill immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Thank you â€” I appreciate that. And could I also request extra towels to be sent to the new room?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Absolutely â€” I'll have them sent up along with a welcome amenity as our apology for the inconvenience.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That's very kind. Thank you for handling this so efficiently â€” I appreciate it.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Of course, Ms Iyer. We want to make sure your stay is as comfortable as possible. I'll arrange for your bags to be moved to Room 608 within the next 15 minutes.

AC not cooling, smoky room, incorrect charge on bill
No hot water, smoky room, wrong floor
